PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/ai-lakehouse/#term","name":"AI Lakehouse","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/ai-lakehouse","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"AI Lakehouse","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/ai-lakehouse"}]}]}See AlsoAI PipelineAuto-regressive ModelsAutoML← Back to DictionaryAI LakehouseTL;DR
An AI Lakehouse extends traditional lakehouse architecture with infrastructure for production AI, including feature stores, real-time inference, model serving, and vector indexing. It unifies analytics and machine learning workloads on a single governed platform.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/sovereign-ai/#term","name":"Sovereign AI","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/sovereign-ai","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Sovereign AI","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/sovereign-ai"}]}]}See AlsoStreaming Feature PipelineStreaming Inference PipelineSchemaSkewSimilarity SearchSQL UDF in Python← Back to DictionarySovereign AIWhat is Sovereign AI?
Sovereign AI is where an organization is capable of building and operating their own AI systems, ensuring data security and compliance with local regulations within the organization’s boundaries. Sovereign AI is often associated with nation states as the organization, implying self-sufficiency and control over a nation’s over AI infrastructure, policies, and data usage.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/ai-pipelines/#term","name":"AI Pipeline","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/ai-pipelines","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"AI Pipeline","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/ai-pipelines"}]}]}See AlsoAI LakehouseAuto-regressive ModelsAutoML← Back to DictionaryAI PipelineWhat is an AI Pipeline?
An AI Pipeline is a program that takes input and produces one or more ML artifacts as output. Typically, an AI pipeline is one of the following: a feature pipeline, a training pipeline, or an inference pipeline.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/auto-regressive-models/#term","name":"Auto-regressive Models","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/auto-regressive-models","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Auto-regressive Models","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/auto-regressive-models"}]}]}See AlsoAI LakehouseAI PipelineAutoML← Back to DictionaryAuto-regressive ModelsWhat are Auto-regressive models?
In many time-series datasets, past values influence the current values. This is true for both stock market prices to words in a sentence or a longer piece of text. An autoregressive (AR) model forecasts future behavior based on past behavior data. AR models are useful for time-series predictions and for language modeling. In both cases, there is a correlation between the preceding and succeeding values in a time-series.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/auto-ml/#term","name":"AutoML","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/auto-ml","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"AutoML","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/auto-ml"}]}]}See AlsoAI LakehouseAI PipelineAuto-regressive Models← Back to DictionaryAutoMLWhat is AutoML?
AutoML stands for Automated Machine Learning, and it describes the process of automating various tasks in model training pipelines, including the autonomous exploration and evaluation of different combinations of model architectures and hyperparameters.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/backfill-features/#term","name":"Backfill features","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/backfill-features","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Backfill features","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/backfill-features"}]}]}See AlsoBackfill training dataBackpressure for feature storesBatch Inference Pipeline← Back to DictionaryBackfill featuresWhat does backfilling features mean?
Backfilling is the process of recomputing datasets from raw, historical data. For reusable features in a feature store, backfilling involves running a feature pipeline with historical data to populate the feature store.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/backfill-training-data/#term","name":"Backfill training data","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/backfill-training-data","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Backfill training data","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/backfill-training-data"}]}]}See AlsoBackfill featuresBackpressure for feature storesBatch Inference Pipeline← Back to DictionaryBackfill training dataWhat does backfilling training data mean?
Backfilling training data from a feature store means creating a point-in-time consistent snapshot of feature data that will be used to train one or more models. Some of the feature data that is used for backfilling may be materialized in the feature store, while some of the feature data may need to be read and computed from external data sources.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/backpressure-for-feature-stores/#term","name":"Backpressure for feature stores","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/backpressure-for-feature-stores","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Backpressure for feature stores","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/backpressure-for-feature-stores"}]}]}See AlsoBackfill featuresBackfill training dataBatch Inference Pipeline← Back to DictionaryBackpressure for feature storesWhat is backpressure for a feature store?
The backpressure pattern consists of a feedback mechanism that allows consumers to inform upstream components when they are ready to handle new messages, preventing them from becoming overwhelmed or stressed. For feature stores, this means that there must be a mechanism to prevent feature pipelines from overloading the feature stores when writing features. In particular, updates by feature pipelines to online feature stores should not affect the performance of clients reading from the same online feature store

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/batch-inference-pipeline/#term","name":"Batch Inference Pipeline","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/batch-inference-pipeline","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Batch Inference Pipeline","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/batch-inference-pipeline"}]}]}See AlsoBackfill featuresBackfill training dataBackpressure for feature stores← Back to DictionaryBatch Inference PipelineWhat is a batch inference pipeline?
A batch inference pipeline is a program that takes as input a batch of data and a model, and outputs predictions that are typically written to some sink, such as a database.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/ci-cd-for-mlops/#term","name":"CI/CD for MLOps","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/ci-cd-for-mlops","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"CI/CD for MLOps","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/ci-cd-for-mlops"}]}]}See AlsoCompound AI SystemsContext Window for LLMs← Back to DictionaryCI/CD for MLOpsWhat is CI/CD for MLOps?
Continuous Integration (CI): CI is the practice of continuously merging code changes from multiple developers into a shared repository, ensuring that the codebase is always up-to-date and consistent. In the context of MLOps, CI involves integrating new ML feature/training/inference pipeline code, updating these ML pipelines. Automated testing of feature/training/inference pipelines and feature functions helps ensure that the integrated changes do not introduce bugs or negatively impact the performance of the ML models.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/compound-ai-systems/#term","name":"Compound AI Systems","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/compound-ai-systems","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Compound AI Systems","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/compound-ai-systems"}]}]}See AlsoCI/CD for MLOpsContext Window for LLMs← Back to DictionaryCompound AI SystemsWhat are Compound AI Systems?
AI systems have long been dominated by single, monolithic models. These models often are massive in size, trained on large datasets, and designed to perform specific tasks. While these single-modeled AI systems have showcased remarkable capabilities, they also revealed significant limitations. They lacked versatility and adaptability and often failed to adjust to dynamic environments.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/context-window-for-llms/#term","name":"Context Window for LLMs","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/context-window-for-llms","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Context Window for LLMs","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/context-window-for-llms"}]}]}See AlsoCI/CD for MLOpsCompound AI Systems← Back to DictionaryContext Window for LLMsWhat is a context window for LLMs?
The context window of LLMs is the number of tokens the model can take as input when generating responses. For example, in GPT-3 the context window size is 2K (2000) and in GPT-4 it is a larger 32K. There is a trend and demand for increasingly larger context window sizes in LLMs. Larger context windows improve LLM performance and their usefulness across various applications.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/dag-processing-model/#term","name":"DAG Processing Model","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/dag-processing-model","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"DAG Processing Model","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/dag-processing-model"}]}]}See AlsoData PipelinesData TransformationData QualityData PartitioningDownstreamData Contract← Back to DictionaryDAG Processing ModelWhat is a DAG processing model?
A DAG (directed acyclic graph) processing model is a method of representing the dependencies between tasks in a workflow or pipeline. In a DAG processing model, tasks are represented as nodes in a directed acyclic graph, where the edges represent the dependencies between tasks. This means that a task can only be executed once all of its dependencies have been completed.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-compatibility/#term","name":"Data Compatibility","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-compatibility","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Data Compatibility","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-compatibility"}]}]}See AlsoData ContractData QualityData Validation (for features)Data Type (for features)Data ModelingDownstream← Back to DictionaryData CompatibilityWhat is data compatibility in ML?
Features are the main data inputs for models for training and inference. Data compatibility often refers to feature consistency, where the schema of features used in feature pipelines, training pipelines, and inference pipelines is compatible - enabling feature pipelines to create features that can then be used without data type errors in training and inference.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-contract/#term","name":"Data Contract","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-contract","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Data Contract","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-contract"}]}]}See AlsoData CompatibilityData QualityData Validation (for features)DownstreamData ModelingData Type (for features)← Back to DictionaryData ContractWhat is a data contract?
A data contract provides schema level guarantees for a feature group and includes metadata such as how/where a feature may be used, the expected freshness of the feature, when it last updated, and optionally the service level agreement (SLA) for lookups.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-lakehouse/#term","name":"Data Lakehouse ","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-lakehouse","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Data Lakehouse ","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-lakehouse"}]}]}See AlsoData WarehouseData PartitioningData ModelingData PipelinesData TransformationDimensional Modeling and Feature Stores← Back to DictionaryData Lakehouse What is a Data Lakehouse?
In the field of data management, data lakes and data warehouses have long dominated the landscape. The data lake, vast and seemingly limitless, promises to hold all your data riches. But venturing in can be treacherous, with information scattered and analysis a challenging task. The data warehouse, on the other hand, offers a meticulously organized oasis, perfect for immediate analysis. However, its rigid structure can leave valuable, unexplored data sources behind.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-leakage/#term","name":"Data Leakage","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-leakage","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Data Leakage","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-leakage"}]}]}See AlsoData QualityData Validation (for features)Data TransformationData-Centric MLData Type (for features)Data Partitioning← Back to DictionaryData LeakageWhat is Data Leakage?
Data leakage occurs when data that should be outside the training dataset is explicitly or implicitly used to train a model. Data leakage can result in the incorrect estimation of the trained model’s performance.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-modeling/#term","name":"Data Modeling","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-modeling","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Data Modeling","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-modeling"}]}]}See AlsoDimensional Modeling and Feature StoresData WarehouseData Lakehouse Data Type (for features)Data PartitioningData Contract← Back to DictionaryData ModelingWhat does Data Modeling for ML mean?
Data modeling describes how the tables in a data warehouse are structured to create a simplified, intuitive, and easy-to-understand layout that enables efficient, ad-hoc querying and analysis of large datasets by business intelligence (BI) users.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-partitioning/#term","name":"Data Partitioning","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-partitioning","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Data Partitioning","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-partitioning"}]}]}See AlsoData Lakehouse Data WarehouseData ModelingData PipelinesData TransformationData Quality← Back to DictionaryData PartitioningWhat is data partitioning and how does it relate to feature stores?
When you create a feature group, you can select one or more features (columns) as the partition key, storing data with the same partition key values in the same directory. Partitioning can enable faster queries using a feature store’s Offline API, by enabling you to pass partition key values, and only results with those partition key values will be read in the query. For example, in Hopsworks, the offline store uses Hive-style partitioning to store feature group partitions in directories. If you only want to create training data for users in t

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-pipelines/#term","name":"Data Pipelines","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-pipelines","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Data Pipelines","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-pipelines"}]}]}See AlsoDAG Processing ModelData TransformationData PartitioningDownstreamData QualityData Contract← Back to DictionaryData PipelinesWhat are data pipelines?
Data pipelines are orchestrated programs that move data from one system to another while also performing transformations on the data either before it has been copied to the target system (ELT) or after it has been copied (ETL). Data pipelines are a key building block in data engineering, as they enable data to flow from operational databases, where the data is generated, to analytical data warehouses, lakehouses, and data lakes where the data is analyzed and used for machine learning. The primary goal of a data pipeline is to automate and streamline the data flow, making it more efficient and reliab

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-quality/#term","name":"Data Quality","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-quality","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Data Quality","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-quality"}]}]}See AlsoData Validation (for features)Data ContractData-Centric MLData LeakageData CompatibilityData Transformation← Back to DictionaryData QualityWhat does high quality data for ML look like?
High data quality for ML refers to data that can be used to train high performance models. Poor training data quality for ML results in models that have low performance, are biased, and cannot generalize.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-transformation/#term","name":"Data Transformation","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-transformation","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Data Transformation","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/data-transformation"}]}]}See AlsoData PipelinesData QualityData Type (for features)Data-Centric MLData PartitioningData Validation (for features)← Back to DictionaryData TransformationWhat is data transformation?
A data transformation is a function that is applied to some input data that changes the data in such a way that the data is easier to consume by downstream applications or users (often business intelligence or ML). Data transformations can take many forms, from simple operations such as filtering or sorting, to complex algorithms such as grouped aggregations, binning, dimensionality reduction, or data cleaning.

Organizations collect data from a multitude of sources, encompassing sales transactions, customer interactions, social media activity, and sensor readings. With the evolution of data science and machine learning (ML) algorithms, this data has become even more important for decision-making. Data-centric ML practices can help iteratively improve a model’s performance by making improvements in the feature data used to train the model. Examples of improvements include adding more (high quality) feature data, adding features that have predictive power, and removing irrelevant/redundant features. Data-centric ML contrasts with model-centric ML that is concerned with iteratively improving model architecture and hyperp

Data modeling for feature stores involves organizing your entities and features into feature groups. In data warehousing, dimensional modeling is a data modeling technique that identifies entities and then decomposes your data into “facts” and “dimensions” related to those entities.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/elt/#term","name":"ELT","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/elt","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"ELT","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/elt"}]}]}See AlsoEmbeddingEncoding (for Features)EntityETL← Back to DictionaryELTWhat is ELT? 
ELT stands for Extract, Load, and Transform of data. ELT is a process whereby a system first extracts data from various sources and then loaded as-is into a data warehouse. Finally, the data is transformed directly in the data warehouse (normally using SQL) into a standardized format suitable for analysis/ML. Compared to the traditional ETL process, where data is transformed in an external engine, like Spark, before it is loaded into the warehouse, in ELT, the data is transformed in-place using SQL in the data warehouse.

An embedding is a compressed representation of data such as text or images as continuous vectors in a lower-dimensional space. Embeddings should capture the semantic relationships and similarities between the original uncompressed objects while reducing the dimensionality of the data.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/encoding-for-features/#term","name":"Encoding (for Features)","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/encoding-for-features","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Encoding (for Features)","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/encoding-for-features"}]}]}See AlsoELTEmbeddingEntityETL← Back to DictionaryEncoding (for Features)What is feature encoding? 
Feature values can be encoded for (1) data compatibility or (2) to improve model performance. For data compatibility, your modeling algorithm may need, for example, to convert non-numeric features into numerical values or to resize inputs to a fixed size. Many deep-learning models have better performance when their numerical input features are standardized - that is, they have a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one.

ETL stands for Extract, Transform, and Load of data. ETL is a process whereby a standalone compute engine, such as Spark, reads data from various data sources (operational databases, message queues, files), transforms the data into a standardized format suitable for analysis/ML, before storing the clean/formatted data to a target data warehouse or lakehouse.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-data/#term","name":"Feature Data","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-data","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Feature Data","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-data"}]}]}See AlsoFeatureFeature ValueFeature TypeFeature GroupsFeature EngineeringFeature Vector← Back to DictionaryFeature DataWhat is Feature Data?
Feature data, often referred to simply as features, is simply the data that is passed as input to machine learning (ML) models. You use feature data both when training a model and when making predictions with a model (inference) - in both cases the input to the model is feature data.  Feature data could be diverse, ranging from numerical attributes like age and price to categorical variables such as hair color or genre preference. Feature data act as the inputs to algorithms, providing crucial information for training ML models. It serves as the cornerstone for building robust models capable of making accurate predictions and

Feature engineering is the process of selecting, creating, and transforming raw data into features that can be used as input to machine learning algorithms. Feature engineering should extract signals from the raw data using domain knowledge to create features that capture relevant information and relationships within the data. Well-designed features can significantly improve a model's performance while also requiring less input data than the raw data.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-freshness/#term","name":"Feature Freshness","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-freshness","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Feature Freshness","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-freshness"}]}]}See AlsoFeature MonitoringFeature PipelineFeature ValueFeature ServiceFeature ViewFeature Groups← Back to DictionaryFeature FreshnessWhat is feature freshness in ML?
Feature freshness refers to the time lag between when the date required to compute a feature becomes available to when the feature is available for use in an inference pipeline.

A feature group is a logical table of features that provides a single API for updating feature values, two different APIs - an online and an offline API - for reading feature values. In practice, feature groups are stored in a dual database system with an online database storing the latest feature values in row-oriented table format  (e.g., OLTP databases or key-value stores) for fast feature retrieval during online inference, and the second offline database stores the historical feature values in a column-oriented store (e.g., data warehouse/lakehouse or object store). The offline

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-logic/#term","name":"Feature Logic","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-logic","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Feature Logic","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-logic"}]}]}See AlsoFeature FunctionFeature EngineeringFeature PipelineFeature ReuseFeature SelectionFeature Groups← Back to DictionaryFeature LogicWhat is feature logic?
Feature logic is the series of steps that transform input data into the unencoded data value that represents the feature in the feature store. The encoding step, which is model-specific, is not typically considered part of the feature logic.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-monitoring/#term","name":"Feature Monitoring","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-monitoring","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Feature Monitoring","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-monitoring"}]}]}See AlsoFeature FreshnessFeature PipelineFeature GroupsFeature ServiceFeature ViewFeature Value← Back to DictionaryFeature MonitoringWhat is feature monitoring?
Feature monitoring involves continuously monitoring the performance of the features used as model inputs in inference pipelines to identify potential problems such as anomalous inputs to models, out-of-distribution inputs for models, and drift in input feature values such that the model performance degrades.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-pipeline/#term","name":"Feature Pipeline","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-pipeline","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Feature Pipeline","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-pipeline"}]}]}See AlsoFeature EngineeringFeature GroupsFeature ViewFeature MonitoringFeature FreshnessFeature Reuse← Back to DictionaryFeature PipelineWhat is a feature pipeline in machine learning?
A feature pipeline is a program that orchestrates the execution of a dataflow graph of feature functions (transformations on input data to create unencoded feature data), where the computed features are written to one or more feature groups. A feature pipeline can also include reading the input data from data sources, data validation, and any other steps needed when computing features.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-platform/#term","name":"Feature Platform","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-platform","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Feature Platform","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-platform"}]}]}See AlsoFeature PipelineFeature GroupsFeature ViewFeature ServiceFeature MonitoringFeature Engineering← Back to DictionaryFeature PlatformWhat is a feature platform?
A feature platform is a feature store that also provides support for a domain-specific language (DSL) to define feature logic and feature pipelines. Feature platforms include an orchestration engine for both feature pipelines and on-demand features.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-reuse/#term","name":"Feature Reuse","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-reuse","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Feature Reuse","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-reuse"}]}]}See AlsoFeature GroupsFeature EngineeringFeature PipelineFeature ViewFeature LogicFeature Selection← Back to DictionaryFeature ReuseWhat is feature reuse?
Features are computed in a feature pipeline and stored in the feature store. Features are reused if the same feature is used in more than one model. That is, the feature is included in one or more feature views, and therefore is included in more than one training/inference pipeline. It is also possible to reuse the same feature logic in one or more feature pipelines, also enabling feature reuse.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-selection/#term","name":"Feature Selection","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-selection","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Feature Selection","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-selection"}]}]}See AlsoFeature EngineeringFeature ReuseFeature GroupsFeature DataFeature ValueFeature Type← Back to DictionaryFeature SelectionWhat is feature selection?
Feature selection is the process of finding existing features, in potentially different feature groups, and joining them together along with the label(s) to define the set of features that will be used for training and inference for your model. Feature selection is performed with respect to a prediction problem that you want your model to solve. The selected features should not be redundant, should also not be prohibited for use, and should be feasible to compute in training and serving.

A feature service is a feature view that is implemented as a network service that provides both an online and offline API for retrieving feature vectors and batches of feature values, respectively. Some feature services require users to provide labels and their timestamps when retrieving features for training data.

A feature type defines the set of valid encodings (model-dependent transformations) that can be performed on a feature value. The standard feature types are categorical (ordinal or nominal), numerical (interval or ratio), and array types (lists or embeddings).

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-value/#term","name":"Feature Value","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-value","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Feature Value","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-value"}]}]}See AlsoFeature DataFeature TypeFeatureFeature VectorFeature GroupsFeature Freshness← Back to DictionaryFeature ValueWhat is a feature value?
A feature value is a measurement (or value) of a feature at a given point in time. For example, if a feature referred to the number of times you logged into a social media site in the previous day, the feature value would be updated once per day and potentially have a different value for each day of the year. There could be up to 365 different feature values for the year.

A feature vector is a row of feature values. A training sample for a model includes a feature vector and the label(s). In inference pipelines, a feature vector is the input values to a model that, in turn, returns a prediction. In online inference pipelines, when you have a feature store, you can retrieve a precomputed feature vector. For this, your online application provides the entity_id ( primary key(s)) that is used by the feature store to return a feature vector of precomputed features for the model. The precomputed feature vector may be joined with on-demand features to produce the final (encoded) featur

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-view/#term","name":"Feature View","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-view","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Feature View","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/feature-view"}]}]}See AlsoFeature GroupsFeature VectorFeature ServiceFeature PipelineFeature EngineeringFeature Monitoring← Back to DictionaryFeature ViewWhat is a feature view?
A feature view is a selection of features (and labels) from one or more feature groups. You create a feature view by joining together features from existing feature groups and optionally performing following steps: defining one or more of the selected features as labels, declaring a transformation (feature encoding) for one or more selected features, and returning only certain feature values by applying a user-supplied filter condition.

Data filtering is an operation on a dataset (such as a DataFrame) that defines which data to extract or remove from the dataset. For example, in a dataset containing all customers in the world, when creating training data, you might only want to read the training data for customers resident in a particular geographical region (e.g., North America, Japan,...), Here, you would define a filter on a region feature when creating training data as shown below.

The fine-tuning of a ML model is when you take a base model with frozen weights, add some new layers on top of the frozen layers, and train the new layers (fine-tune the model as a whole) using your own training data that is specific to the task you want to use your model for.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/flash-attention/#term","name":"Flash Attention","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/flash-attention","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Flash Attention","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/flash-attention"}]}]}See AlsoFine-Tuning LLMsFunction Calling with LLMsFeature EngineeringFeature PipelineFeature PlatformFeature Service← Back to DictionaryFlash AttentionWhat is Flash Attention?
Flash Attention is a method to improve the efficiency of transformer models, in particular large language models (LLMs), helping reduce both model training time and inference latency. Inference latency is, in particular, a challenge for LLMs, and flash attention has become a key technique that enables your LLM applications to respond faster.

In the realm of large language models (LLMs), Function Calling refers to the ability of a LLM to impute, from the user prompt, the correct function to execute from a set of available functions and the correct parameters to pass to that function. Instead of generating standard text responses, a LLM for function calling is typically fine-tuned to return structured data responses, typically JSON objects. The returned structured data can be used to execute predefined functions, such as retrieving data from a data wareh

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/generative-ai/#term","name":"Generative AI","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/generative-ai","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Generative AI","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/generative-ai"}]}]}See AlsoGradient AccumulationGrouped Query Attention← Back to DictionaryGenerative AIWhat is generative AI?
Generative AI generally refers to models and techniques that generate new data samples by learning the underlying data distribution. Examples of generative models include Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs). These models learn to generate new samples by capturing the inherent structure and patterns in the training data. ChatGPT and GPT-4 are examples of generative models.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/gradient-accumulation/#term","name":"Gradient Accumulation","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/gradient-accumulation","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Gradient Accumulation","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/gradient-accumulation"}]}]}See AlsoGenerative AIGrouped Query Attention← Back to DictionaryGradient AccumulationWhat is Gradient Accumulation? 
Imagine you have to fine-tune a LLM, but you only have a small number of GPUs, making your training memory-constrained. Or imagine you want to train an image classifier but you don't have enough GPU memory. In these cases, Gradient Accumulation can help. Gradient Accumulation is a technique used when training neural networks to support larger batch sizes given limited available GPU memory.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/grouped-query-attention/#term","name":"Grouped Query Attention","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/grouped-query-attention","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Grouped Query Attention","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/grouped-query-attention"}]}]}See AlsoGenerative AIGradient Accumulation← Back to DictionaryGrouped Query AttentionWhat is Grouped Query Attention?
Large language models (LLMs) are revolutionizing various domains. However, their power comes at a cost – they can be computationally expensive, especially during the inference stage, where the model applies its learned knowledge to new tasks. This is where techniques like grouped query attention (GQA, first introduced in the paper “GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models from Multi-Head Checkpoints”) come in.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/hallucinations-llms/#term","name":"Hallucinations in LLMs","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/hallucinations-llms","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Hallucinations in LLMs","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/hallucinations-llms"}]}]}See AlsoHyperparameterHyperparameter Tuning ← Back to DictionaryHallucinations in LLMsWhat are Hallucinations in LLMs?
Hallucinations are responses by LLMs that appear to be correct but are actually false or not based on the input given. Hallucinations can occur when you give a LLM a prompt that is ambiguous or can be interpreted in more than one way or when the LLM has not been trained on enough training data.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/hyperparameter/#term","name":"Hyperparameter","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/hyperparameter","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Hyperparameter","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/hyperparameter"}]}]}See AlsoHallucinations in LLMsHyperparameter Tuning ← Back to DictionaryHyperparameterWhat are hyperparameters in machine learning?
In training pipelines, a hyperparameter is a parameter that influences the performance of model training but the hyperparameter itself is not updated during model training.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/hyperparameter-tuning/#term","name":"Hyperparameter Tuning ","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/hyperparameter-tuning","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Hyperparameter Tuning ","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/hyperparameter-tuning"}]}]}See AlsoHallucinations in LLMsHyperparameter← Back to DictionaryHyperparameter Tuning What is hyperparameter tuning in machine learning? 
Hyperparameter tuning involves training multiple models each with different hyperparameter values to find good values for hyperparameters that optimize model performance.

An idempotent operation produces the same result no matter how many times you execute it. Idempotent operations can be retried after failure without worrying about side-effects, meaning they are important in building reliable, self-healing systems.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/in-context-learning-icl/#term","name":"In Context Learning (ICL)","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/in-context-learning-icl","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"In Context Learning (ICL)","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/in-context-learning-icl"}]}]}See AlsoIdempotent Machine Learning PipelinesInference DataInference LogsInference PipelineInstruction Datasets for Fine-Tuning LLMs← Back to DictionaryIn Context Learning (ICL)What is In Context Learning (ICL)?
In-context learning (ICL) is a specific method of prompt engineering where demonstrations of the task are provided to the model as part of the prompt (in natural language). With ICL, you can use off-the-shelf large language models (LLMs) to solve novel tasks without the need for fine-tuning. ICL can also be combined with fine-tuning for more powerful LLMs.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/inference-data/#term","name":"Inference Data","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/inference-data","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Inference Data","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/inference-data"}]}]}See AlsoIdempotent Machine Learning PipelinesIn Context Learning (ICL)Inference LogsInference PipelineInstruction Datasets for Fine-Tuning LLMs← Back to DictionaryInference DataWhat is inference data?
Inference data is the input feature values that are the input to a trained model that outputs a prediction.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/inference-logs/#term","name":"Inference Logs","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/inference-logs","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Inference Logs","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/inference-logs"}]}]}See AlsoIdempotent Machine Learning PipelinesIn Context Learning (ICL)Inference DataInference PipelineInstruction Datasets for Fine-Tuning LLMs← Back to DictionaryInference LogsWhat are inference logs?
Inference logs are the input and output of inference pipelines. Where possible, inference logs should be stored as rows, where each row includes the unencoded feature values and the corresponding model prediction. Inference logs are required by both feature and model monitoring.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/inference-pipeline/#term","name":"Inference Pipeline","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/inference-pipeline","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Inference Pipeline","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/inference-pipeline"}]}]}See AlsoIdempotent Machine Learning PipelinesIn Context Learning (ICL)Inference DataInference LogsInstruction Datasets for Fine-Tuning LLMs← Back to DictionaryInference PipelineWhat is an inference pipeline?
An inference pipeline is a program that takes input data, optionally transforms that data, then makes predictions on that input data using a model. Inference pipelines can be either batch programs or online services. In general, if you need to apply a trained machine learning model to new data, you will need some type of inference pipeline.

Instruction datasets are used to fine-tune LLMs. Fine-tuning LLMs typically uses supervised machine learning and includes both an input string and an expected output string. The input and output string follow a template known as an instruction dataset format (e.g., [INST] <<SYS>>). ChatML by OpenAI and Alpaca from Stanford are examples of Instruction Dataset Formats. The following is the instructi

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/kserve/#term","name":"KServe","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/kserve","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"KServe","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/kserve"}]}]}See AlsoModel ServingModel DeploymentInference PipelinevLLMMachine Learning Systems← Back to DictionaryKServeWhat is KServe?
KServe is an open-source model serving platform that operates on Kubernetes. KServe excels in scalability, enabling the deployment of a wide variety of ML models as network-accessible services on a Kubernetes clusters including Tensorflow, XGBoost, Scikit-Learn, PyTorch, ONNX, and large language models (LLMs).

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/lagged-features/#term","name":"Lagged features","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/lagged-features","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Lagged features","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/lagged-features"}]}]}See AlsoLangChainLatent SpaceLLM Code InterpreterLLM TemperatureLLMOpsLLMs - Large Language Models← Back to DictionaryLagged featuresWhat are lagged features?
Lagged features are a feature engineering technique used to capture the temporal dependencies and patterns in time series data. A lagged feature is created by taking the value of a variable at a previous time point and including it as a feature in the model at the current time point. This is done by shifting the time series data by a certain number of time steps, which is referred to as the lag or time lag.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/langchain/#term","name":"LangChain","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/langchain","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"LangChain","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/langchain"}]}]}See AlsoLagged featuresLatent SpaceLLM Code InterpreterLLM TemperatureLLMOpsLLMs - Large Language Models← Back to DictionaryLangChainWhat is LangChain?
LangChain is an open source framework, for Python or Javascript, designed to simplify the writing of applications using LLMs. LangChain enables developers to design templates for prompts for LLMs, like GPT-4, and fill in those templates with external data, such data that comes from a vector database or feature store. Examples of applications built with LangChain include document analysis and summarization, chatbots, code analysis, and source code generation.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/model-performance/#term","name":"Model Performance","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/model-performance","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Model Performance","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/model-performance"}]}]}See AlsoModel MonitoringModel Evaluation (Model Validation)Model TrainingModel ArchitectureModel InferenceModel Quantization← Back to DictionaryModel PerformanceWhat is model performance in machine learning?
Model performance in machine learning is a measurement of how accurate predictions or classifications a model makes on new, unseen data. You typically measure model performance using a test set, where you compare the predictions on the test set to the actual outcomes. For classification, you can use accuracy as a measure of model performance, but you cannot use accuracy as a measure of model performance for regression problems, where metrics such as mean absolute error and (root) mean squared error are used.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/model-quantization/#term","name":"Model Quantization","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/model-quantization","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Model Quantization","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/model-quantization"}]}]}See AlsoModel InferenceModel ServingModel DeploymentModel PerformanceModel ArchitectureModel Training← Back to DictionaryModel QuantizationWhat is model quantization?
Model quantization can reduce the memory footprint and computation requirements of deep neural network models. Weight quantization is a common quantization technique that converts a model’s weights from the standard floating-point data type (e.g., 32-bit floats) to a lower precision data type (e.g., 8-bit integers), thus saving memory and resulting in faster inference (through reduced computational complexity). Model quantization can make large models, such as LLMs, more practical for real-world applications at edge devices.

A model registry is a version control system for models that provides APIs to store and retrieve models and model-related artifacts. A model registry stores different versions of models, including metadata about their performance metrics, author, creation date, dependencies, usage, and lineage (training experiment code/hyperparameters and /training data).

Model serving refers to the process of deploying and making ML models available for use in production environments as network invokable services. In simpler terms, it's about taking a trained ML model and making it accessible for real-world applications via a REST or gRPC API. In particular, model serving involves setting up an infrastructure or system that can host ML models and handle network requests to the ML model and provide predictions in real-time.

Model-centric ML is an approach to machine learning that focuses on iteratively improving model architecture and hyperparameters to enhance model performance. This method emphasizes the importance of finding the best model architecture, configuration, and hyperparameters to achieve the desired level of accuracy and generalization for a given task.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/mvps/#term","name":"MVPS","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/mvps","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"MVPS","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/mvps"}]}]}See AlsoMachine Learning SystemsMLOpsModel DevelopmentModel DeploymentMachine Learning PipelineMachine Learning Infrastructure← Back to DictionaryMVPSWhat is a MVPS?
A MVPS is a Minimal Viable Prediction Service. It is the simplest end-to-end ML system that includes the three main ML pipelines: a feature pipeline, a training pipeline, and an inference pipeline and uses a feature store and model registry as the shared data layer. A MVPS should show stakeholders how a model will AI-enable some existing or new service in the environment in which the model will be used. A MVPS should also show how the model performance is related to the business KPIs that the prediction service should improve.

NLP stands for Natural Language Processing. NLP is concerned with how computers understand, interpret, and generate human language, both in written and spoken form. NLP is used in ML Systems and online inference pipelines as part of the user interface (both input and output). In feature pipelines, NLP is used for language translation, sentiment analysis, text classification, speech recognition, and information extraction.

If a feature is used in an online inference pipeline and it is created using data only available at request-time, then it is an on-demand feature. If you have historical data, you would like to be able to create the same feature values in your feature pipeline using the same code as is used in the online inference pipeline (to avoid online-offline skew).

An on-demand transformation is a feature function that is used to compute an on-demand feature. It is run both in an online inference pipeline and in a feature pipeline. In the online inference pipeline, the on-demand feature function takes as input data only available at request-time (it may also use pre-computed features from the feature store), while in the feature pipeline the on-demand feature function takes as input historical data.

PricingBlogDictionaryResourcesSovereign AIStart Free{"@context":"https://schema.org","@graph":[{"@type":"DefinedTerm","@id":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/parameter-efficient-fine-tuning-of-llms/#term","name":"Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) of LLMs","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/parameter-efficient-fine-tuning-of-llms","inDefinedTermSet":{"@type":"DefinedTermSet","name":"MLOps Dictionary","url":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"}},{"@type":"BreadcrumbList","itemListElement":[{"@type":"ListItem","position":1,"name":"Home","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":2,"name":"MLOps Dictionary","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary"},{"@type":"ListItem","position":3,"name":"Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) of LLMs","item":"https://www.hopsworks.ai/dictionary/parameter-efficient-fine-tuning-of-llms"}]}]}See AlsoPrompt TuningPrompt EngineeringPagedAttentionPrompt StorePython UDFPandas UDF← Back to DictionaryParameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) of LLMsWhat is Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) of LLMs?
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) enables you to fine-tune a small subset of parameters in a pretrained LLM. The main idea is that you freeze the parameters of a pre-trained LLM, add some new parameters, and fine-tune the new parameters on a new (small) training dataset. Typically, the new training data is specialized for the new task you want to fine-tune your LLM for (e.g., for the clinical domain).

That is, a point-in-time correct join is a temporal join between two tables where the output reflects the state of the both tables at a specific point in time defined by timestamps from the left table (containing the labels). A precomputed feature is a feature that has been created by a feature pipeline and is stored in a feature store. Training and inference pipelines can read precomputed features and use them directly for training or inference. Precomputed features are typically stored unencoded if they are intended for reuse across multiple models, but they can also be stored encoded if an online model has low latency SLAs and encoding the feature at runtime is too expensive.

Prompt engineering involves designing natural language queries (prompts) to produce desired results from a LLM. The prompt is the input text to your LLM, and the response of your LLM is the output. The goal of prompt engineering is to maximize the effectiveness, accuracy, and usefulness of the responses from the LLM. The prompt is often a statement or question that is given in English (or another language) as model input. How a prompt is structured influences the output of the model. For example, a LLM might respond differently to two very similar prompts: "R

Large language models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for generating creative text, answering complex questions, and completing various tasks. However, the potential of LLMs and the quality of the responses LLMs generate are highly associated with the prompts users provide. This is where prompt stores come into play. It is a log of prompts and responses from LLMs, acting as a bridge between human intention and LLM capabilities.

Prompt tuning (PT) is a parameter-efficient adaptation method for LLMs that adds a small number of tunable embeddings to an otherwise frozen model. Prompts do not have to be written in natural language (e.g., English). In prompt tuning (or prefix-tuning), floating-point-valued vectors are searched directly by gradient descent, to maximize the log-probability of outputs.

In ML, a Python UDF (user-defined function) is a function written by a user, typically to implement a feature function. Python UDFs are popular for feature engineering as they can be applied to a Pandas DataFrame to transform it into one or more features. Python UDFs are flexible and allow users to define custom transformations, but they can be slower than vectorized Pandas UDFs due to the overhead associated with Python's interpreted nature.

A schema defines the shape, order, and type of data stored in ML artifacts, including:  feature groups, feature views, training datasets, and models. The schema enables the validation of the shape, order, and type of data that is either read from or input to a ML artifact. Some ML artifacts, such as feature groups, feature views, and models can support schema versioning. A schema version change indicates that the new version has a breaking schema change compared to the previous version.
